data.title: Xcel Energy: Buy This Dividend Aristocrat In The Making
data.description:
This article was coproduced with Kody Kester.

We highly value companies that value shareholders. Please allow me (Kody) to explain further.

For newer readers and as a reminder to more established readers, we like to see the following attributes from the companies we highlight:

  1. Tangible growth catalysts. Businesses with clear tailwinds can't help but generally grow their earnings, which makes them more valuable over time.
  2. The balance sheet must also be investment-grade. This is because an investment-grade balance sheet affords a company a lower cost of capital. That can further support a company's growth plans.
  3. The business must have a reputation for consistent dividend growth and a manageable payout ratio. This shows a track record of prioritizing shareholders. Combined with the other characteristics, that often translates into ever-growing dividends.

We believe Xcel Energy (XEL) fulfills these requirements. When we last covered XEL with a buy rating a few months ago, we liked that it had matched or topped earnings guidance for 20 consecutive years. The company's five-year capital spending plan was encouraging. Its financial health was fine, too. XEL was also on pace to become a Dividend Aristocrat in a few more years. Shares were also undervalued by a double-digit percentage to our fair value estimate.

Fast forward to today, and the story remains much the same, with a bit more reason for optimism. XEL posted robust Q2 results. The company also upped its additional investment pipeline from $10 billion-plus to $15 billion-plus. XEL's balance sheet remains intact. The stock is still moderately discounted as well.

Xcel Energy Delivered A Strong Q2

This week, XEL shared its earnings report for the second quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The company's operating revenue rose by 8.6% over the year-ago period to $3.29 billion in the quarter. That came in ahead of the analyst consensus by $80 million.

What was behind XEL's top line growth during the second quarter?

As was also the case in the prior quarter, the regulated utility benefited from customer growth and higher usage for the second quarter. The former varied from 0.7% for SPS to 1.3% for PSCo in the quarter. The latter ranged from 0.2% for NSP-Minnesota to as much as 5% for SPS during the quarter.

XEL's business model also accounted for some of its operating revenue growth for the second quarter. This is because its regulated utility structure allowed it to pass higher electric fuel/purchased power and natural gas costs onto customers in the quarter.

XEL's ongoing diluted EPS soared 38.9% year-over-year to $0.75 during the second quarter. That exceeded the analyst consensus for the quarter by $0.11. Fuel cost recovery, sales and demand, and non-fuel riders for electric revenues, allowance for funds used during construction debt and equity, and natural gas revenues lifted ongoing diluted EPS. That more than neutralized greater electric fuel and purchased power costs, interest charges, natural gas costs, and depreciation and amortization.

Looking out over the next several years, the outlook for XEL has arguably become even better.

When we last covered it, the company had a $45 billion five-year capital spending plan for 2025 through 2029. That base capital plan alone was expected to generate a 9.4% annual growth rate in the rate base. The base capital plan remains unchanged.

However, XEL's forecast for additional capital investment (mostly over that period) is up from the prior figure of $10 billion to $15 billion now. In his opening remarks during the Q2 2025 earnings call, Chairman and CEO Bob Frenzel outlined several drivers of this incremental need.

XEL plans to invest an incremental $3 billion to $4 billion in regional transmission projects to support reliability and regional growth. The company also secured approval for two firm dispatchment projects totaling 720 megawatts and at least an additional 2,800 megawatts of company-owned wind in Minnesota.

As a reminder, XEL anticipates that every $1 billion of incremental capex would grow its rate base by around 25 basis points.

So, it's not hard to see how the company could deliver on the high end or even beat its 6% to 8% annual ongoing diluted EPS growth target. Analysts as a whole tend to agree with this assessment.

The FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that ongoing diluted EPS will rise by 9.1% in 2025 to $3.82. An additional 8.1% jump in ongoing diluted EPS to $4.13 is expected for 2026. In 2027, another 8% increase in ongoing diluted EPS to $4.46 is the current consensus.

XEL is also in an enviable financial position. The company anticipates that its FFO-to-debt ratio will hold at around 17% through at least 2029. This is enough to warrant Baa1 (BBB+ equivalent), BBB, and BBB+ credit ratings from Moody's, S&P, and Fitch, respectively (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to XEL's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release and XEL's Q2 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value Has Surpassed $80 A Share

Since May, XEL has returned 5% as the S&P 500 index (SP500) returned 14% during that time. Still, XEL has matched the S&P so far this year, posting a roughly 10% total return.

We believe that the regulated utility isn't finished rallying, either. Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.4. According to data from FAST Graphs, that's moderately below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 20.4.

We also think that a reasonable fair value P/E ratio for XEL remains the 10-year average of 20.4. This is because XEL's 8.1% annual forward ongoing diluted EPS growth consensus remains considerably higher than the 10-year average of around 6%. In addition to our analysis of the fundamentals earlier, this builds a convincing case that the regulated utility's growth picture has never been stronger.

We would continue to argue that this offsets the valuation headwind that would otherwise arise from higher 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yields. Historically, XEL's earnings yield has offered a 200 to 250 basis point spread to the 10-year yield.

Moving forward, we believe that this could narrow to 100 to 150 basis points (it already has in recent years, which reflects the improved growth profile). Plugging that in with our assumption of a 10-year yield in the high 3% range, this is how we come out to our fair value P/E ratio of 20.4.

Currently, the calendar year 2025 is 58% complete. That leaves another 42% of 2025 and 58% of 2026 yet to come over the next 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month ongoing diluted EPS input of $4.

Applying our fair value multiple of 20.4, we compute a fair value of $82 a share. Against the current $74 share price (as of July 31, 2025), this works out to be a 10% discount to fair value.

If XEL matches the growth consensus and returns to fair value, it could generate a 14% total return by the end of June 2026. Through 2030, the annual total return potential would be 11% in such a case.

The Dividend Has More Room To Run

XEL's 3.1% forward dividend yield clocks in a bit below the utility sector's 3.4% forward median yield. That accounts for C grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively.

From a dividend consistency and safety perspective, XEL is arguably a standout dividend stock.

The company has upped its dividend for 22 consecutive years. This easily bests the sector median of just 11.2 years. That's sufficient for the Quant System to award an A grade for overall dividend consistency.

There's also no indication that the company will stop growing the payout anytime soon. This is evidenced by the fact that XEL's ongoing diluted EPS payout ratio is set to be around 60% in 2025. For more perspective, that's less than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry, per The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal. This explains the B- grade for overall dividend safety from the Quant System.

On the dividend growth front, XEL is respectable as well. The Quant System anticipates 4.9% annual forward dividend growth. That would be comparable to the sector median of 5.2%. Given that XEL has no shortage of investment opportunities and the payout ratio is on the high end of its targeted range, we think this is realistic. This is why the Quant System issues a C grade for the forward dividend per share growth metric.

Risks To Consider

XEL's catalysts remain in place to drive vigorous growth in the future. However, the regulated utility still has risks that could materially alter the investment thesis.

As we outlined in our prior article, the biggest risk to XEL arguably remains the concentration of its rate base in Colorado and Minnesota. We don't have the latest data from the upcoming 10-Q filing on the proportion of its rate base in these two geographies (NSP-Minnesota does include some customers in the Dakotas, too). Overall, we'd expect that these markets still comprise around three-quarters of its total rate base, though.

Thus, XEL's fundamentals largely hinge on Colorado (the larger rate base of the two) and Minnesota. Any adverse rate case outcomes in those states could weaken the company's financial metrics and diminish the risk profile.

That could warrant credit rating downgrades from the big rating agencies. This would likely interfere with XEL's ability to deliver on its growth ambitions.

Additionally, the company faces pronounced wildfire risks in Texas and Colorado. A report from Verisk Wildfire Analytics ranks these two states as the second and third most dangerous states for wildfires, behind only California.

XEL's implementation of wildfire mitigation plans in these states should help to somewhat balance out the risk. If any severe wildfires happen, however, the company could be on the hook for legal liabilities.

One final risk to XEL is the potential for a major cyber breach. The company has been a target of attempted hacks. If any are successful, that could jeopardize sensitive customer information. This could also lead to litigation being brought against XEL.

Summary: Xcel Energy Is A Buyable SWAN Stock

We remain bullish on XEL. The company had a solid second quarter. It also significantly upped its incremental investment opportunities forecast over the next five years. That provides it with plenty of ways to generate growth for the foreseeable future. XEL remains financially sound, too.

Finally, the stock is trading at a valuation that's less than our fair value estimate. This gives it decent upside in the near-term and the medium term. Thus, we're reiterating our buy rating.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Trump Signs New Tariff Order
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump issues revised tariff orders. (00:21) Reddit's (RDDT) results topper, robust forecast drive shares higher. (01:49) Palantir (PLTR) lands enterprise deal worth up to $10B from U.S. Army. (02:58)

This is an abridged transcript.

President Trump on Thursday signed an executive order revising “reciprocal” tariffs on dozens of countries, setting new duty rates between 10% and 41%.

According to the White House, any goods deemed to be transshipped to evade these duties will incur an additional 40% tariff.

Syria faces the highest tariff at 41%. Switzerland and South Africa will see rates of 39% and 30%, respectively, while shipments from Taiwan will face a 20% tariff, lower than the 32% set earlier.

Trump also proceeded with his plan to raise tariffs on Canadian imports to 35% from 25%, effective August 1, 2025.

We have a detailed A-Z list of countries and territories with their respective reciprocal tariffs in this article in the show notes section of the show.

The revised tariff rates will take effect for goods “entered for consumption on or after 12:01 a.m. Eastern Daylight Time, seven days after the order’s issuance,” unless otherwise specified. That would make it August 7th.

Countries not included in the latest order will be subject to a 10% duty, according to the directive. The updated order revises tariffs set under the earlier executive order from April.

China faces an August 12 deadline to finalize a lasting tariff agreement with the Trump administration, following preliminary deals struck in May and June.

Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) is up 15% in premarket action.

The social media company provided a strong forecast and reported a double beat for its second-quarter earnings.

For the third quarter, Reddit expects revenue between $535M and $545M and adjusted EBITDA between $185M-$195M. These numbers surpass the Bloomberg consensus estimate of $472.7M for revenue and $159.5M for adj. EBITDA.

In Q2, Reddit saw daily active unique users rise 21% from last year to 110.4M, ahead of the 109.75M estimate.

Net income for the three months ended June 30 was $89M, compared with a loss of $10M a year ago.

On a per-share basis, the California company earned 45 cents, ahead of the average analyst estimate of 19 cents.

Total revenue surged nearly 78% to $500M beating the consensus estimate. Advertising revenue was up 84% at $465M; revenue from other sources was $35M, up 24%.

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) has secured a major deal from the U.S. Army worth up to $10B over the next decade, which establishes a framework for the Army's future software and data needs.

The new enterprise agreement consolidates 75 contracts (15 prime contracts and 60 related contracts) into a single one. It’ll enhance military readiness, drive operational efficiency, and deliver cost efficiencies.

The deal establishes volume-based discounts for the contract's performance period of up to 10 years. The Army and other Department of Defense agencies have the option to buy Palantir's (NASDAQ:PLTR) commercial products during that period, not to exceed the $10B cap.

The Washington Post reported, the award is one of the biggest DoD contracts ever, and is the largest ever awarded to Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR).

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Amazon posts a powerhouse quarter again; shares fall as guidance is weighed

Apple rises after 'solid' Q3, as Wedbush says iPhone China-beat 'star of the show'

Trump issues 60-day ultimatum for big pharma to lower drug prices

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 0.8% at $68/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.5% at $115,000. Gold is up 0.1% at $3,294.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.5% and the DAX is down 1.7%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Figma (NYSE:FIG) +11% – Shares extended gains after a stunning NYSE debut on Thursday, where they soared 250%. The design software firm priced its IPO at $33 per share which is now worth $115.50.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Employment Situation

  • 10:00 am ISM Manufacturing Index

data.title: Builders FirstSource: Q2 Results Point To Further Weakness (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Builders FirstSource (BLDR) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing about a quarter of their value as the company has been hurt by weakening residential construction. Shares were flattish Thursday as investors digested weak quarterly earnings, suggesting that housing has yet to hit bottom. Even with this fall, shares are actually still up 15% since I last covered them in May. At that time, I rated BLDR a “hold,” and the stock’s performance has matched the market. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if a hold rating remains appropriate. I am more bearish.

In the company’s second quarter, Builders FirstSource earned $1.66, which was $0.26 below expectations as revenue declined to $4.2 billion, about $80 million lower than consensus. Adjusted EPS was $2.38, which was still down 32% from last year. BLDR is facing weaker demand from homebuilders given a softening housing market, and resultant excess capacity is leading to further margin pressure.

In Q2, gross margins contracted by 210bps to 30.7% resulting in an 11% drop in gross profit to $1.3 billion. Margins were up 20bps sequentially, but based on guidance, this will be short-lived with further margin compression looming. Despite declining revenue, SG&A grew by 0.5% to $818 million, as aggressive M&A has increased its operating footprint and increased costs. As a result of this, EBITDA declined by 24% to $506 million with margins at 12%, and free cash flow was down 31% to $255 million.

Over the past few years, BLDR has used windfall profits to diversify its business away from commodity lumber exposure, which does make strategic sense to me as that should over time reduce the volatility of earnings and give BLDR more control over its P&L. Value-added products accounted for 47% of sales, up 2% from 4 four years ago, as a result of this effort.

However, it is not having the desired effect. Value-added organic sales were down by 11% given lower housing starts while lumber was down 6%. Including M&A, manufactured products declined by 10% to $953 million while windows and doors fell by 8% to $1.03 billion. In other words, the commodity-centric part of its business is actually holding in better, even as lumber pricing was down 1.5%, a $34 million headwind.

This is because even its value-added products are exposed to home construction activity. As you can see, the single-family construction market accounts for the lion’s share of BLDR’s business. I would also note that while multifamily is a small part of the business it will still drag $500 million on sales this year, though most of this occurred in H1 as multifamily began slowing more aggressively during 2024.

The challenge BLDR faces is that fewer homes are under construction. With declining prices across the Sun Belt, builders are pulling back. While lumber demand is high shortly after construction commences, value-added products like doors and windows are generally installed a bit later in the construction process. This is why the ongoing decline in the number of homes being built is beginning to hits its value-added segment harder. This trend is also well entrenched, and while the speed of the decline is uncertain, it seems highly likely that fewer homes will be under construction in 3, 6, and 12 months from today. In other words, the business has not hit bottom yet.

Indeed, as you can see below, housing starts have fallen sharply this year. With starts running lower, that means fewer homes will be entering the “under construction” phase. This will be a headwind for all aspects of the business. With mortgage rates unlikely to fall sharply, I struggle to see a significant turnaround in homebuying activity given challenging affordability levels. As such, we are likely to see builders continue to pull back on new supply until the market finds a clearer equilibrium.

Interestingly, on the earnings call, management said the housing market is worse than homebuilders have acknowledged on their earnings calls and in public statements. Now, BLDR does operate significantly in the markets that are facing the most pressure like Florida, Texas, and Arizona, and that may also be contributing to BLDR’s pessimistic take. Management is now forecasting single-family starts to be down 10-12%. It previously expected housing starts to be down mid-single digits.

Because of this, management slashed guidance with the high end of sales now $450 million below the previous low-end. Similarly, EBITDA sits below the entire previous range. Management only trimmed the top-end of its free cash flow guidance, given it is also slashing cap-ex to preserve cash flow.

It is also important to note that while Builders FirstSource’s balance sheet used to be a source of strength, that no longer is the case. While BLDR still describes leverage as “low,” it is above its 1-2x target, and with EBITDA continuing to decline from last year, this metric will continue to rise through year end. Importantly, it does not have maturity before 2030. In other words, its balance sheet is not strong but is not a real problem either.

Given the market downturn, I expect that BLDR will be comfortable running leverage a bit above target, and I still view sub-2.5x as reasonable. While the company did $391 million of share repurchases, these were largely in April. With leverage more likely to approach 3x, I expect a significant slowdown in buybacks in H2. It is possible that BLDR continues to halt repurchases, and M&A remains a higher capital priority, though in this environment, I believe BLDR should be cautious about over-expanding. Due to buybacks, there has been a 7.5% share count reduction over the past year.

Back in May, I saw the company generating $8-$8.25 in free cash flow and felt that the stock would be worth $130 if the US avoided a recession. As recession fears have faded, that is indeed where the stock traded at. At the midpoint of guidance, this company has about $8-$8.10 of free cash flow capacity. In other words, revised guidance really just brings BLDR’s view in line with my own existing expectations.

I am cautious that we may see further degradation in housing and expect BLDR to come in at the lower half of guidance, or $7.80-$8.10/share of free cash flow. I am more concerned that the pace of recovery will be substantially slower. The current number of houses under construction essentially supports near-term revenue, but with mortgage rates likely stuck in the 6.5-7.25% into next year in my view, I struggle to see housing construction bottoming within the next 12 months. Plus, with a weaker balance sheet, the amount of free cash flow it can return to investors next year is likely to narrow.

At $125, shares now have a roughly ~6.3% free cash flow, broadly consistent with my prior fair value estimate. However, I do not see free cash rising until 2027 at the soonest, and I believe investors may be disappointed with the pace of recovery and 2026 capital returns, especially when many homebuilders have better balance sheets and ~10% capital return capacity. As such, I see shares resuming their fall and pushing back to the $100-110 area, or closer to and 8% free cash flow, reflecting a weaker balance and slower recovery. With shares up 15% since May, now is a time for investors to exit BLDR. I view BLDR as a "sell."

data.title: Webull: Valuation Stretched, Profitability Uncertain; Sell
data.description:
Introduction

A newer trading platform that has been rising in status—Webull (NASDAQ:BULL), which allows for ETFs, stocks, options, and other assets to be traded around 14 global markets. The company recently started trading publicly and has allowed for rapid growth and more investor attention. However, even with its high momentum, I believe the current valuation is disconnected from fundamentals. This is due to its extreme multiples, thin profitability, and too much optimism on future growth assumptions. In this report, I will go over financial metrics, comparable companies, and DCF modeling to demonstrate the value of Webull and explain why I assign a sell rating.

Company Overview

Webull generates revenue through:

  1. Commissions: come from payments from options and futures
  2. Interest income: specifically on customer cash balances, security lending, and margining.
  3. Subscriptions and other services: "Webull Premium" which is a paid membership that offers advanced tools.

To add, Webull holds customer deposits and records large cash balances and liabilities ($4.32 billion in liabilities, $2.07 billion in assets), demonstrating margin financing rather than debt.

Recent Financial Performance

Webull's consolidated financial statements for FY 2024 show that the company was losing money on an accounting basis.

Even though they had a large net loss, Webull had positive operating cash flow and FCF. This is not surprising, as brokerage operations allow for an increase in working capital. Even so, negative equity is a sign that their business is still in "investment" mode.

First quarter 2025

On May 22, 2025, Q1 was released. Here are some highlights:

Revenue: Revenue grew 32% YoY ($117.4 million), and trading-related revenue increased 52%.

Operating cost: Operating expenses decreased to 2% to $96.8 million.

Profitability: Income before taxes went from -$9.8 million to $19.5 million. Adjusting for operating profit was $28.7 million (24.4% operating margin).

Customer metrics: Registered users rose to 24.1 million (+17%) and funded accounts to 4.7 million. Assets of customers grew 45% ($12.6 billion), and net deposits rose 66%.

Profitability Trajectory

Q1 results demonstrate increasing growth and Webull's first profitable quarter (under GAAP). This is a positive outlook for some investors who like to look far into the future—if this type of growth continues, Webull could be able to sustain profitability very soon.

Cash Flow

FY 2024 revenue was only $389 million; FCF margins are low (around 2.6%). This is all very low for a platform with millions of registered accounts—a problem we will touch upon later, specifically the richness of the accounts created. This limits the amount of operating cash it can generate without margin expansion.

Growth Levers

Revenue, which is dependent on trading volumes, has been declining in stock lending, mostly due to sensitivity in market behavior. Webull has been pivoting away from "free stock" promotions to reduce acquisitions while increasing deposit quality. This is where they would basically give free stock to users for depositing (a certain amount) and creating an account on Webull. Although it would bring in high user volumes, many of the accounts that were created were low value (small or inactive deposits and minimal engagement). Therefore, Webull has to now focus on a different approach so they can increase average deposit size and user quality. As a result, they are now shifting to gain more from margin lending, premium services, and interest income from cash balances.

For example, across Reddit, there are some listings of free stock promotions (most were delisted from a while ago, hence reddit)

What Does This Mean Going Forward?

If user growth persists, keeping momentum strong (all while expenses remain in control), Webull could be in a position to have constant positive profitability. To add, the company is able to generate strong operating cash flow (even when equity has been negative historically).

Expansion has been continuing, with Webull trying to position themselves as a full-service software—with diversification into bonds, crypto, mutual funds, and retirement accounts.

But still take all of this with a grain of salt. Investor sentiment is cautious, not only due to its overall valuation but also because it's a new company to the market. For me, it is hard to have faith in Webull right now, as we need to see future earnings.

DCF Valuation

Using a 15% FCF margin on FY 2024 revenue, a WACC of 7.5%, 5‑year growth of 5%, and a terminal growth rate of 2%, the DCF gives an intrinsic value of about $2.65 per share. This reflects the higher risk of a young fintech company and the high overvaluation that the market is now placing on the stock itself. In order to calculate this, I started with Webull's FY 2024 revenue of $434.6 million, as reported in its 20-F filing with the SEC (Webull 20-F). Applying a 15% free cash flow margin yields approximately $65.2 million in the base year FCF (conservative compared to brokerages like Robinhood, 12-20% margins in mature periods, and Interactive Brokers, 30%+). The 15% comes from middle ground, factoring in Webull's reliance on interest income, margin lending, and low-cost operations.

For WACC, we estimate the cost of equity using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM): Cost of Equity = Risk-free rate + Beta × Market risk premium = 4.2% (10-year Treasury) + 1.2 × 8% = 13.8%

Since Webull has no long-term debt and large cash reserves, we assume a nearly 100% equity capital structure. After adjusting for beta closer to 0.9-1.0 (due to fintech volatility) and applying tax effects, we arrive at a WACC of approximately 7.5%.

Next, we project FCF forward over five years at a 5% annual growth rate, which is a conservative assumption compared to Webull's historical user and revenue growth but more realistic as the company matures. This gives us Year 5 FCF of approximately $79.3 million. Using a 2% terminal growth rate (aligned with long-run global GDP estimates), the terminal value is calculated using the Gordon Growth Model:TV = FCF in Year 5 × (1 + g) / (WACC - g) = 79.3 × 1.02 / (0.075 - 0.02) = 80.9 / 0.055 = $1.47 billion

Discounting the five years of FCF and the terminal value at 7.5% WACC yields a total enterprise value of approximately $1.29 billion. Based on an estimated 139.3 million shares outstanding and adjusting for dilution and negative common equity (as reported in Webull's filings), we arrive at an intrinsic equity value of roughly $2.65 per share.

Even with a 7% discount rate, the price per share hits a max of $3.20. This DCF analysis suggests two things: first, the current market price already embeds considerable expectations for future growth, and secondly, until Webull has more predictable FCF, value estimates will continue to be broad.

Multiples

Just looking at valuation multiples alone demonstrates that the company is entirely priced on future expectations.

When looking for online sources for the P/E ratio, it's actually really hard to find and is listed as NM (not meaningful) across all quarters due to the lack of positive earnings. Most people would get confused thinking that it's low, but rather the fact is it doesn't even exist yet. What is even more concerning is when we look at EBT and normalized EPS, we see extreme valuations. The Market Cap/LTM EBT Excl. Unusual Items multiple rose sharply to 277.14× on June 30, 2025, and 287.17× on July 30, 2025. At the same time, the P/LTM normalized EPS is sitting at a very high 171.61×. This is beyond what you would see for a hypergrowth SaaS (typically between 25× and 40×).

Their market cap/LTM revenue sits at a multiple of 16.26× to 20.60× in the latest quarters, which is well above the norms for fintech peers (closer to single digits). This reflects extreme optimism about future revenue growth and demonstrates a huge vulnerability. For example, let's say revenue growth slows and users don't buy premium services—the stock price will end up dropping at a very fast pace.

From one angle, Webull can be seen as a growing platform with strong user acquisition and positive sentiment. But from another perspective, profitability is still too thin to justify such high enterprise value multiples, leaving very little room for incorrections.

All in all, Webull is risky but a high-reward growth play. If management does well, grows EBITDA significantly, and improves margins, today's multiples could normalize, validating the premium. But the downside is that if growth slows or profitability lags, the market could quickly reprice the stock.

Comparable Companies

Webull is competing in the market with other commission fee/low-fee brokerage platforms. Two of the most useful comparable companies are Robinhood (HOOD) and UP Fintech Holding Limited (TIGR).

Robinhood

Most people might know Robinhood from the infamous GameStop incident back in early 2021, but it is also one of the first companies to allow for commission-free trading, ETFS, American depository receipts, options, gold, and cryptocurrencies.

Up Fintech

A Singapore-headquartered online brokerage firm founded in 2014. They are also known as Tiger Brokers in Asia. They provide investment services to retail and institutional clients worldwide, with a primary focus on Chinese investors in markets such as New Zealand, the U.S., Singapore, and the Cayman Islands.

General Observations

Scale

Robinhood's far exceeds Webull's funded accounts (23 M vs. 4.7 M), and what's even more of an eye-catcher is its $255 billion of platform assets compared to Webull's $12.6 billion assets. Even with UP Fintech having fewer users than Webull, they still have $45.9 billion dollars of assets. The $45.9B in platform assets demonstrate a much higher average asset per user (which is around $40,000). This means there are more affluent international clients who are active in cross-border investing. This edge allows TIGR to drive higher interest income and margin lending revenues with fewer users.

Market Cap/Revenue

Robinhood's Market Cap-to-Revenue multiple is around 24-29× (Jul 2025), which signals that the market is pricing in dominant scale and high-growth potential. Webull trades at 13-16×, which is a somewhat similar level but without the same scale or assets under management (AUM). TIGR, by contrast, trades at only 4.4-4.8×, reflecting more profitable revenue per dollar. So while Webull trades at multiples comparable to Robinhood, it lacks the size and maturity; this looks extremely expensive when compared to TIGR on a revenue-adjusted basis.

Market Cap/EBT (excluding unusual items)

Robinhood's market cap to EBT multiple is approximately 64-69×, which is high but at least reflects some level of profitability. Webull's multiple, however, is a staggering 287×, showing that its earnings before tax are almost non-existent relative to its valuation. TIGR trades at a far more reasonable 15-16×, signaling stronger operational efficiency. This makes clear that Webull is the least profitable of the three and that investors are relying heavily on future growth to justify its current valuation.

P/E Multiples

Webull's P/E trades at NM (Not Meaningful), which looks cheap at first glance but is misleading due to lack of positive earnings. Robinhood sits at around 53-62×, which is definitely expensive, but demonstrates its scale and higher growth rate. TIGR is around 19-22×, demonstrated and justified by steady profit growth and high AUM per user.

Strategic Positioning

Robinhood has a heavy U.S. brand with a large AUM and is still priced as a long-term platform play despite high multiples. Webull, on the other hand, which isn't much different than Robinhood as a platform, has a tiny AUM/user and is valued at a premium but has weak fundamentals vs. peers. TIGR, the smallest but with wealthier clients, is a profitable cross-border niche and shows margin quality.

Risks

Platform Trust and Brand Fragility

As known, Webull is still building its brand and is not really known when compared to Robinhood or Charles Schwab. Especially with a younger user base and less brand loyalty—if the platform faces, for example, order execution errors, poor customer support, or security concerns, it could end badly for the company.

Limited Feature Differentiation

Webull offers little difference to its peers. I would say areas such as social trading, advisor integration, or even educational content. As trading becomes commoditized and more competitors enter the market—Webull risks being another "Robinhood clone."

Customer Concentration in Short-Term Traders

The userbase of Webull is more so (young) high-frequency traders who often switch platforms based on promotions and the interface. This means that this type of demographic is not likely to hold long-term assets or retirement accounts—making each customer's value lower than for platforms like Fidelity or Schwab.

Reliance on Payment for Order Flow (PFOF)

Webull earns most of its revenue through PFOF. Any future restriction or ban on PFOF will change Webull's monetization and will force them to find new revenue streams.

Lack of Ecosystem

Webull has no such integration between different financial products or tools, severely limiting its cross-potential and making it harder to raise more revenue. Other applications have these types of cross-products through financial instruments, making Webull have much less of an edge.

Catalysts for Growth

Margin Expansion: If Webull is able to expand specifically through premium features or even higher-value trading services, their margins will expand drastically.

Regulatory Approval for Retirement Accounts: If they are able to create infrastructure for IRAs and 401(k) offerings—this will open a wide range of clients (and in particular wealthier) that will stick with their company.

Global Expansion: Being able to move into markets such as Latin America or Southeast Asia, they could be able to drive growth that is far more lucrative than the US.

Additional Considerations: Management, Monetization, and Regulatory Outlook

Management & Leadership Execution

Leadership is under the radar when compared to other fintech companies. The company's CEO, Anthony Denier, has a background in finance but lacks any record of creating and managing startups. Still, while the platform has had major growth (in user terms), it is hard to see if the team has the operating power to work through potential regulatory shocks, capital constraints, or global expansion hurdles.

Weak Monetization Funnel

Even though they have over 24 million users, Webull's strategy to keep these users is still in development. They make most of their money through payment for order flow and margin lending (which lacks diversification). This taken into account makes the company very sensitive to shifts in the market and overall market sentiment.

Regulatory Risks Beyond PFOF

Payment to order flow is the biggest concern I have—especially with SEC Chair Gary Gensler's scrutiny. Any ruling that affects order execution disclosers, data privacy, or their interface could force Webull to make very expensive platform adjustments.

Conclusion

To put it shortly, Webull is high risk - high reward. The company is still growing rapidly, and its fundamentals are not able to back up its current premium price. When we look at comparable companies such as Robinhood and TIGR (which either have a greater scale or profitability), Webull still trades at higher multiples.

Without having more data to look at their financial statements or having constant user growth, it's hard to place Webull in a position to be a player in the market. This is why I believe it is best to sell any existing positions in Webull.

data.title: BTC Revaluation Masks A Tough Q2 For Riot Platforms
data.description:
A little over 5 months ago, I covered Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT) for Seeking Alpha and declared the stock a viable trading ticker, but probably not something investors should hold for a long period of time. In the time since that piece, RIOT has actually performed reasonably well—beating both the broader market and the CoinShares Bitcoin Mining ETF (WGMI) over the last five and a half months:

While holding RIOT stock has not been as lucrative as simply owning Bitcoin (BTC-USD) either directly on-chain or through the ETF wrapper, RIOT has beaten the market since mid-February. In this update, we'll look at key takeaways from the company's Q2-25 earnings report, the pivot from HODL-ing to selling production over the last three months, and whether Bitcoin mining profitability has improved with six-figure BTC prices.

Q2-24 Earnings

For the quarter ended June 2025, Riot Platforms reported $153 million in top-line revenue. While this was a 5% sequential decline from $161.4 in Q1, revenue was up 118% year-over-year from the same period in 2024.

Revenue (000s) Q2-25 Q2-24 YoY
Bitcoin Mining $140,889 $55,764 152.7%
Engineering $10,576 $9,627 9.9%
Other $1,523 $4,627 -67.1%
Total $152,988 $70,018 118.5%
BTC % of Total 92.1% 79.6% 15.6%

Source: Riot Platforms, 10-Q

Though Riot announced HPC data center intentions earlier this year and has subsequently hired a 'Chief Data Center Officer' to further those efforts, the company was even more reliant on Bitcoin mining for top line revenue in Q2-25 than it was in Q2-24; at $140.9 million in revenue from Bitcoin mining, Riot garnered over 92% of the company's revenue in the quarter from BTC. This was a 15.6% year-over-year increase from just under 80% BTC reliance in Q2-24.

Engineering revenue came in at $10.6 million - up just shy of 10% year over year. 'Other' was down. The point is, the company still generally goes as Bitcoin goes, and the mining space continues to be an incredibly challenging business:

RIOT did indeed report positive net income of $219.5 million in the quarter, but this was entirely dependent on a $470.8 million revaluation of Riot's enormous BTC treasure trove. Meaning, without increases in the value of the Bitcoin that Riot Platforms had already mined in prior quarters, the company's true net income would have almost certainly been negative in Q2, even with BTC in six-figure territory.

Even if we were to take away a $158.1 million loss on a contract settlement and a $42.7 million change in value of derivative assets, RIOT would have still been negative in the quarter by over $50 million. It took $250.1 million in combined COGS, SG&A, and depreciation/amortization to generate $153 million in revenue.

Bitcoin Treasury Shift

What I do find to be quite interesting over the last three months is the fact that Riot shifted from HODLing all production for 13 straight months between February 2024 and March 2025. The company pivoted to selling most of its monthly production in April and continued that behavior through June:

Over the last three months, Riot has realized $131.8 million in BTC sales. Prior to HODLing all production beginning in February of last year, Riot had been selling some of its monthly mining rewards through the entirety of 2023. To get a sense of how substantial this Bitcoin revenue has been for Riot between April and June, it took all of January through October 2023 for the company to reach the realized BTC sales revenue level it reached in Q2 nominally.

Measured in BTC, it took 5,055 BTC to generate $131.5 million in realized revenue between January and October 2023. The company generated $131.8 million in realized sales from selling just 1,372 BTC last quarter. Despite the massive jump in average sales price from $26k to $96k per BTC in our sample above, it still wasn't enough to generate positive adjusted net income, taking away asset revaluation.

The Mining Business is Still Brutal

The true economics of mining continue to be quite poor when depreciation/amortization are included as all-in costs. Hash price remains near the new floor post-halving:

This chart is the reason why Bitcoin miners haven't had the same parabolic pop that they had during the 2021 bull run. The daily revenue expected from mining per PHS is less than $60, and that has generally been the case since the block reward halving in April 2024. The fact that the BTC price has gone as high as $120k per coin hasn't alleviated margin pressure to the degree mining stock investors likely would have hoped. Global exahash continues its relentless rise:

More importantly, the hash rate is growing in line with Bitcoin's price. Thus, the explosive mining stock price rallies that we saw during the 2021 bull cycle - which generated immense profitability numbers due to global exahash not growing as quickly—have not manifested this time around.

Closing Remarks

During the last cycle, miners selling down their BTC stacks was an early warning that trouble was coming for the broader industry. While RIOT isn't tapping the company's BTC treasury stack yet, the fact that the company has shifted to selling nearly all monthly production for an entire quarter might be a signal worth considering. To be sure, data center Capex will need funding, and Riot's options are some combination of dilution, debt, and/or monetizing BTC. The latter of which might actually be the most prudent way to fund data center growth.

It's also interesting that Riot Platforms has begun reducing its holdings of Bitfarms (BITF) stock in recent weeks. Many will likely remember the failed takeover attempt of Bitfarms by Riot last year. That Riot is now reducing its Bitfarms position is not exactly a ringing endorsement for the current state of the BTC mining industry from where I sit. All this said, I'll reiterate my 'hold' for RIOT. If the price of BTC continues to rise, RIOT should benefit due to how much coin the company holds. However, I'm becoming increasingly convinced that we're close to BTC's high for the year if we haven't seen it already.

data.title: AI Monthly: From Experimentation To Integration - Solving The AI Scale-Up Challenge
data.description:
By Diederik Stadig and Amrita Naik Nimbalkar

Building a use case takes time

According to Bain data covering February, July, and December 2024, adoption of generative AI use cases has accelerated across nearly all enterprise functions. Software development, customer service, knowledge work, and marketing lead the way, with development or pilot activity close to, or above, 60%. It is no surprise, then, that capital commitments by AWS (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Meta (META), and Microsoft (MSFT) are expected to amount to more than $300 billion this year, more than double the amount in 2023.

Still, production deployments lag significantly - deployment percentages are often less than half the corresponding pilot rates. In sales, for instance, fewer than 15% of companies have AI tools running at scale, but over 30% are in the pilot stage. Legal and HR functions show even wider gaps, with less than 10% in production. The sheer volume of pilot programmes suggests that few firms can afford to stay on the sidelines, but that many firms face organisational challenges in implementing AI at scale.

Pilot-deployment gap shows difficulty in deploying AI at scale

Enterprise use case adoption for generative AI in December 2024

Messy corporate structures can prevent AI implementation

A paper by Ruthanne Huising shows why it can take time to build a use case. Her paper follows teams tasked with mapping out their company’s internal operations. It reveals that many were struck by the surprising complexity and opacity of corporate processes and decision-making. As participants created these maps, from raw materials to finished products, they discovered outputs that were produced for no one, duplication of work, and non-hierarchical ways of getting work done. When presenting these maps to chief executives, participants found that executive boards often lacked knowledge of these design and operational issues. This, in turn, led to a sense of disillusionment among the mapping teams, as they came to realise that even chief executives can struggle to fully grasp the inner workings of their organisations.

In organisational theory, this is often dubbed the ‘Garbage Can Model’. It represents a mode of corporate organisation where complex and undocumented ways of doing things are key. In such a situation, it is hard to implement novel technologies such as AI, because the implementation of AI requires clear processes and rules, especially at scale.

Employee scepticism may also contribute to the deployment gap

While the ‘Garbage Can’ model might help to explain the delayed scale-up of AI in organisations, employee perceptions about AI can also hinder progress. According to the European Commission’s Eurobarometer survey, an average of 62% of the respondents in the European Union have a positive view of AI and robots in the workplace, yet 84% believe they require careful management. When it comes to decision-making, a little over half consider robots and AI suitable for making accurate decisions, and almost three-quarters support prohibiting fully automated decision-making by AI systems and robots. This suggests that while employees may rely heavily on AI for their day-to-day tasks, many remain unconvinced of its reliability in high-stakes or large-scale applications, potentially hindering broader adoption.

AI implementation at scale requires organisational readiness

For investors and policymakers, this divergence between invention and implementation presents both risks and opportunities, raising questions about utilisation efficiency, pricing power, and long-term monetisation. Overcoming the pilot-deployment gap will not only require technical readiness, but also organisational clarity, process transparency, and employee trust. As the data shows, enthusiasm is not the issue; execution is.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Western Asset Inflation-Linked Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: During the second quarter, increased volatility was marked by geopolitical shocks and macro crosscurrents. The market saw bouts of risk-off sentiment, most notably following the "Liberation Day" tariff announcement on April 2 and the brief U.S., Iran and Israel conflict. Markets subsequently rebounded as most tariffs were delayed, and Iran and Israel agreed to a tentative ceasefire. The front end of the U.S. Treasury (UST) yield curve declined, while long-end yields rose on concerns of rising deficits and potential inflation reacceleration. Throughout the quarter, credit spreads tightened and emerging market (EM) local yields fell. The S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index returned 10.57%, and the U.S. dollar weakened versus most EM and developed market (DM) currencies.
  • Contributors: Interest rate positioning and sector exposures contributed to performance.
  • Detractors: The Fund's Treasury Inflation-Protected securities (OTCPK:TIPS) exposure detracted from performance.
  • Outlook: Global growth is expected to slow given heightened unpredictability, but should remain positive. Overall monetary policy remains restrictive and we believe that central banks will continue to cut rates. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. While fundamentals remain positive, spreads are at the tight end of historical ranges in some sectors and warrant caution.

Performance Review

  • Interest rate positioning had a positive impact on performance as the yield curve steepened.
  • Emerging market exposures were additive to returns as U.S. dollar-denominated spreads tightened.
  • In addition, corporate credit exposures (both investment-grade and high- yield) were additive for results, benefiting from positive issue selection and tightening spreads.
  • Structured products exposure in aggregate had a positive impact as spreads generally tightened.
  • In terms of detractors, Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (OTCPK:TIPS) exposure was a headwind for returns.

Outlook

  • U.S. government policy has caused severe volatility in fixed-income markets over the last several months. Global growth is expected to slow given heightened unpredictability, but should remain positive. U.S. growth is downshifting due to a myriad of factors, including tariff uncertainty, waning benefits from immigration, and reduced government spending in recent years. A significant fiscal boost from European defense and German infrastructure spending should support eurozone growth and provide relief from tariff-related uncertainty. Deflationary pressures in China persist and confidence is weak amid property market concerns, but sentiment is improving with fiscal stimulus and policy easing.
  • Overall monetary policy remains restrictive and we believe that central banks will continue to cut rates. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen further given concerns over fiscal policies.
  • While fundamentals remain positive, spreads are at the tight end of historical ranges in some sectors and warrant caution. We will continue to look for further periods of volatility to add to spread products.

Benchmark(s)

Performance shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance shown. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so shares, when sold, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Returns based on Market Price or NAV, and assume the reinvestment of all distributions at the Dividend Reinvestment Plan Price or NAV, respectively. All returns include the deduction of management fees, operating expenses and all other fund expenses, and do not reflect the deduction of brokerage commissions or taxes that investors may pay on distributions or the sale of shares. When applicable, performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expense or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. Please visit franklintempleton.com for the most recent month-end performance.

Gross expenses are the fund's total annual operating expenses as of the fund's annual report available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect voluntary fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements. Voluntary waivers may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

The difference between total assets and net assets, if any, is due primarily to the fund's use of borrowings and other liabilities; net assets do not include borrowings. The fund may employ leverage in the form of loans, preferred stock, reverse repurchase agreements and/or other instruments. When the fund engages in transactions that have a leveraging effect on the fund's portfolio, the value of the fund will be more volatile and all other risks will tend to be compounded.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. The fund is subject to the additional risks associated with inflation-linked securities, including liquidity risk, prepayment risk, extension risk and deflation risk. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Currency management strategies could result in losses to the fund if currencies do not perform as expected. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Commodity-related investments are subject to additional risks such as commodity index volatility, investor speculation, interest rates, weather, tax and regulatory developments. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks. Leverage increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects investments to magnified losses and a decline in value.

Glossary

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow (DOW) Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved.

The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager's assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund's portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Closed-End Funds are not sold or distributed by Franklin Distributors, LLC, or any affiliate of Franklin Resources (BEN), Inc. Unlike open-end funds, shares are not continually offered. Like other public companies, closed-end funds have a one-time initial public offering, and once their shares are first issued, are generally bought and sold through non-affiliated broker/dealers and trade on nationally recognized stock exchanges. Share prices will fluctuate with market conditions and, at the time of sale, may be worth more or less than your original investment. Shares of exchange-traded closed-end funds may trade at a discount or premium to their original offering price, and often trade at a discount to their net asset value. Investment return, market price and net asset value will fluctuate with changes in market conditions. The Funds are subject to investment risks, including the possible loss of principal invested. Distributions are not guaranteed and are subject to change.

The Bloomberg U.S. Government Inflation-Linked 1-10 Year Index measures the performance of the intermediate U.S. TIPS market. Source: Bloomberg Indices. The Bloomberg U.S. Government Inflation-Linked All Maturities Index measures the performance of the U.S. TIPS market. The Index includes TIPS with one or more years remaining maturity with total outstanding issue size of $500 million or more. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at Third Party Content Providers and Disclosures. All data is subject to change.

Effective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes.

Credit Quality is a measure of a bond issuer's ability to repay interest and principal in a timely manner. The credit ratings shown are based on each portfolio security's rating as provided by Standard and Poor's, Moody's (MCO) Investors Service and/or Fitch Ratings, Ltd. and typically range from AAA (higheSt) to D (lowest), or an equivalent and/or similar rating. For this purpose, if two or more of the agencies have assigned differing ratings to a security, the highest rating is used. Securities that are unrated by all three agencies are reflected as such. The credit quality of the investments in the fund's portfolio does not apply to the stability or safety of the fund. These ratings are updated monthly and may change over time. Please note, the Fund itself has not been rated by an independent rating agency.

© Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC. Western Asset Management Company, LLC, and Franklin Distributors, LLC, are Franklin Templeton companies.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights. Please read it carefully.

data.title: U.S. Fed Keeps Interest Rates On Hold Despite Political Pressure To Cut
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Reserve kept its key interest rate on hold at a range between 4.25-4.5% for the fifth straight meeting. Scott Colbourne, Managing Director & Head of Active Fixed Income, TD Asset Management, speaks with MoneyTalk’s Anthony Okolie about the latest decision and the potential rate path ahead for the Fed.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: The US Federal Reserve has held its overnight rate steady for a fifth straight meeting, keeping it at a range of between 4 and 1/4% and 4 and 1/2%. Joining me now to discuss is Scott Colbourne of TD Asset Management.

And Scott, thanks for joining us. As expected, the Fed kept its interest rate on hold despite a lot of political pressure for aggressive cuts. What stood out for you today?

Scott Colbourne: Well, I mean, the market wasn't expecting them to cut, so that was pretty much as expected. We did see the two dissents. One was really telegraphed earlier from Waller-- so that largely as expected. I think we're focusing on how the Fed is characterizing inflation, employment, and the economy. And for the most part, the Fed has characterized today, in its statement, that inflation remains somewhat elevated. The labor market remains solid.

But it did acknowledge that we're seeing some moderation in the economy. And so that is leading us towards the end of the year into the September meeting and where we go from there. Certainly, there is lots of political pressure, and we see that regularly.

Anthony Okolie: OK, I want to talk about those dissents, because, as you mentioned, the central bank's decision was not unanimous. Two Fed governors dissented from the policy call for the first time since 1993. What are the implications for monetary policy going forward?

Scott Colbourne: Well, I know right after the Fed announced today's press release, President Trump did say, I hear they're cutting in September. So it's intense. The political pressure is very focused. The markets have about a 2/3rd chance of a Fed cut priced in right now. So we're going to be getting data between now and then that will allow the Fed to shape its decision.

I think, really, what you see in terms of the bond market is the shape of the yield curve, the combination of the fiscal aspect of issuance of debt, plus the pressure in what we would largely characterize as a fairly good employment market-- 4.1% unemployment. A moderating economy, yes, but inflation is somewhat sticky as well.

And so, in that sort of mixture, you wouldn't be expecting the Fed to cut. So, this really affects the shape of the yield curve with a steepening bias, where we see the long end underperforming the front end of the yield curve.

Anthony Okolie: I want to talk a little bit about the economy, because so far the US tariffs are not having that inflationary impact that many economists had feared. There is some stickiness to it. But what do you attribute that to? And are calls for a rate cut warranted?

Scott Colbourne: Well, I mean, I think the Fed pushed back today on that imminent political pressure. And what we see is-- tariffs take time. The number in January, the average tariff rate was around 2-3%. It's now up closer to 17% in July.

It's going to take time for tariffs to pass through. The indications are 6-18 months is sort of a pass-through time. We've seen some evidence in CPI that some of the goods prices are a little bit stickier. Inflation swaps markets-- the markets that the bond market focuses in on-- inflation remains elevated.

So I think, net-net, the market is cognizant that there is pressure. We'll continue to see pressure into the balance of this year into the new year. So they're pushing back against this narrative, particularly as Governor Waller articulated, that it's a one-time pass-through effect of inflation. The market is saying, show me the evidence. It's not there. And I think that's the tension and the debate that is evident in the Fed right now.

Anthony Okolie: OK, let's switch over to talking about the tax bill, the one Big Beautiful Tax Bill which was signed into law earlier this month. That's raising concerns over government spending. What are the implications for Fed policy and bond markets going forward, given that this bill has now passed?

Scott Colbourne: Yeah, the pressure from the fiscal side or the stimulus side is that it's predominantly on the shape of the yield curve and how the Treasury manages the issuance. Today, we had the refunding announcement, largely as expected for the sixth consecutive quarter. Issuance is about as expected.

There was some indication that we got a little bit of a buyback program going on in the 10-year and out part of the yield curve, maybe a nudge towards the Treasury acknowledging that there's going to be pressure on 10- and 20- and 30-year bonds because of the fiscal issuance, because of the stimulus on the fiscal side. So, I think the Treasury is aware, and I think we'll see into September, into the balance of the year, the Treasury will get one more crack at managing the shape of the yield curve through the issuance.

Anthony Okolie: OK, let's talk about the US dollar. I want to get your outlook on the dollar, which seems to be kind of caught between two opposing forces. We've got the resilient economy and trade deals on one side against the political interference and uncertainty over the Fed's next move. Where do you see the US dollar going forward?

Scott Colbourne: Yeah, I mean, I would characterize what we've seen this year as a dialing back of the US exceptionalism story, and it fed through into the activities of global asset managers, fast money, real money, sovereign wealth funds. And they hedged more of their US dollar exposure. A lot of this was unhedged. That is out of the way now.

So now we're at the cross just as-- both sides of the coin are out there, right? Fed rates on hold. There is a bounceback in the equity market. So, US dollar assets are performing well. But there is this political pressure and this desire for the political administration to have a weaker dollar. So that tension's at play. It's a little bit more of a balanced story right now. Ultimately, I do think that the US dollar weakness story plays out. But right now, we're in that in-between point in the market.

Anthony Okolie: OK, so as we await the next Fed decision, which will be in September, what will you be watching closely to get a sense of what the Fed's next move will be?

Scott Colbourne: In the very near term, this Friday, we have the nonfarm payrolls. So that will give us an indication. The Fed characterized the labor market today as solid. So, do we see any more cracks in the labor market? And then between now and September, which is when the Fed is going to next meet and produce the summary of economic projections, they will have two employment data and two CPI.

And I think, even if the whole debate in, there's not complete clarity, I think it gives enough evidence to the Fed to either stay on hold for the balance of the year or start cutting.

Original Post
